actions.label: Epic Life Membership
actions.description: A done-for-you wealth concierge — a team that runs your financial playbook every month (protection, credit, banking, your tax-free policy, then passive income). Because they do the work, enrolling never costs you energy or one of your monthly moves — their action runs on top of yours each turn. ⚠️ First game? Try a run without it.
actions.lesson: The wealthy don't run the whole financial playbook themselves — they hire specialists who run it for them. A concierge trades a monthly fee for time and flawless execution: the protective and wealth-building moves happen on schedule whether or not you remember them. (This is a real service — the game just lets you feel the value.)
actions.narrative_success: Welcome to Epic Life. Your concierge team is on it — from here the financial playbook runs in the background. Watch for the 🌟 Epic Life updates each month as each piece clicks into place.

actions.label: Form Your LLC
actions.description: Form your LLC, open a business bank account, and set up bookkeeping — making your business a real legal entity with clean, separate finances.
actions.lesson: Separate yourself from the business from day one. The LLC shields your personal assets, the business account stops commingling, and bookkeeping lets you prove income to lenders — the foundation everything else is built on.
actions.narrative_success: The LLC is filed, the EIN issued, a business checking account is open, and your books are live. Your business exists as its own legal person with clean financials — and bookkeeping runs about $150/mo from here.
actions.narrative_failure: The LLC is filed and the account is open, but the bookkeeping setup is half-done — categories are a work in progress. Still running about $150/mo.

actions.label: Build Business Credit (D&B)
actions.description: Get a D-U-N-S number, business phone/address/website, and net-30 vendor accounts so lenders see business credit, not just your personal score.
actions.lesson: Lenders check your BUSINESS credit (D&B / Paydex), not just your personal score. A DUNS number, a real address and phone, a web/social presence, and vendor tradelines that report on-time payments build a business credit identity — so the company borrows on its own name, off your personal guarantee. Each business credit line you add deepens the file too.
actions.narrative_success: D-U-N-S number issued. Business phone, address, website and socials are live, and three net-30 vendors are reporting your on-time payments. Your D&B profile is officially building — and climbs as the tradelines age and you add credit history.
actions.narrative_failure: Paperwork delays on the DUNS registration held things up — resubmit next month.

actions.label: Build Personal Credit
actions.description: Dispute errors and clear collections to rebuild your score over a few months — and your card issuer may automatically raise your limit, which lowers utilization without you paying anything down.
actions.lesson: Building credit is mechanics, not magic. The biggest levers are on-time payments and low utilization — keep card balances well under 30% of the limit. Disputing genuine errors and resolving collections helps too. Start early if you're carrying collections; the cleanup takes a couple of months to show.
actions.narrative_success: You pulled all three reports, challenged the genuine errors, knocked down a couple of balances, and put a plan against the collections. The corrections take 30-90 days, and you'll watch your score climb month over month as the file cleans up.
actions.narrative_failure: Mixed-up paperwork stalled the disputes and one collection wouldn't budge — only a little movement this month. Run it again to keep the cleanup going.

actions.label: Pay Down Debt
actions.description: Use spare cash to pay down revolving balances toward the 30% utilization sweet spot — the fastest lever on your credit score.
actions.lesson: Utilization (how much of your revolving limit you're using) is one of the biggest levers on your credit score — get it under 30%. Don't rush to kill cheap installment loans; that money is better kept working unless your debt-to-income is also too high.
actions.narrative_success: Two thousand dollars gone from the balance. The interest savings alone will compound from here.
actions.narrative_failure: Paid down most of it. An unexpected expense ate part of the payment. Still progress.

actions.label: Build a Banking Relationship
actions.description: Open a business account, run your deposits through their bank, and meet your banker regularly — so you're a known, trusted customer before you ever need a loan. Banks lend bigger lines, and approve far more readily, to people who already bank with them.
actions.lesson: Capital is a relationship business. Bankers fund people they know and trust — open the account, keep deposits flowing, and build the relationship BEFORE you need money. When you do apply, you'll qualify more easily and for larger amounts than a stranger walking in cold.
actions.narrative_success: The branch manager knows your name now. When you need capital, you won't be a stranger walking in cold.
actions.narrative_failure: Good meeting, but the banker transferred branches. You're starting the relationship over with someone new.

actions.label: Take a Term Loan
actions.description: Apply for a fixed-rate term loan — personal-sized before your LLC, larger and business-sized after — to fund growth or consolidate debt.
actions.lesson: Debt isn't the enemy — UNPRODUCTIVE debt is. Borrowing to grow a business that pays you back is how you buy freedom in years instead of decades. A salaried W-2 paycheck is taxed at ordinary rates with almost no deductions and no control over timing — business owners get deductions, structures, and leverage an employee never does.
actions.narrative_success: Approved for $10,000. The loan officer barely hesitated — your credit spoke for itself. Cash in account by Friday.
actions.narrative_failure: Declined. The loan officer said your debt-to-income was borderline. Try paying down some debt first.

actions.label: Open a Business Credit Line
actions.description: Open or stack a revolving credit line in the business name — cash on demand, lower utilization, and dry powder ready when you need it.
actions.lesson: Business credit borrows on the company's name and assets — not yours. This is how you scale with other people's money without putting your household on the line. Each added line lowers utilization and sits ready as dry powder — access is an asset even when idle.
actions.narrative_success: Approved for $15,000. The banker shook your hand and said 'come back when you need more.' You will.
actions.narrative_failure: Declined — the bank pulled your personal credit and didn't like what it saw. The hard inquiry dinged your score a couple points. Pay down utilization and reapply.

actions.label: Restructure Your Debt
actions.description: The full debt playbook in one move: drop utilization, shift balances onto business credit (off your personal report), and pull working cash.
actions.lesson: The optimal debt move in one sequence: revolving → installment drops your utilization, then moving it onto business credit takes it off your personal report entirely, and the 0% intro buys time. Same debt, restructured into a higher score and usable cash.
actions.narrative_failure: The expert moved one balance and opened a starter business line, but couldn't restructure everything yet. Your file is building — reapply in a month for better terms.

actions.label: Elect S-Corp
actions.description: Put yourself on payroll, adopt an operating agreement, and elect S-Corp taxation to legally cut your self-employment tax.
actions.lesson: How your income is STRUCTURED matters as much as how much you earn. An S-Corp election splits your pay into a salary plus distributions — only the profit ABOVE a 'reasonable salary' escapes self-employment/payroll tax. The catch: the IRS requires that salary be genuinely reasonable; lowball it and you invite an audit. Done right, it's a lever a W-2 employee never gets, and the operating agreement hardens your liability shield.
actions.narrative_success: You're on payroll, the operating agreement is signed, and the S-Corp election is filed. Your tax advisor estimates $8,000–$12,000 in annual savings — and lenders now see a real, bankable business.
actions.narrative_failure: Payroll is running and the election is filed, but at a lower salary than planned. Savings are real, just smaller until revenue catches up.

actions.label: Set Up a Holding Company
actions.description: Stand up a Wyoming holding LLC above your business — registered agent, business address, and a real operating agreement. A clean parent entity that owns the valuable pieces, shields them from operating liabilities, and makes banks take you seriously.
actions.lesson: A holding company owns; the operating company does the risky work — so a lawsuit or bad debt against operations can't reach what's held above it. Wyoming adds strong charging-order protection and privacy. Pair it with clean fundamentals underwriters look for — a proper operating agreement, registered agent, and the CORRECT NAICS industry code on every entity (a mismatched code on any business can get you flagged or declined). Structure first, then leverage.
actions.narrative_success: The Wyoming holding LLC is filed — registered agent in Cheyenne, operating agreement executed, NAICS code assigned. It sits above your operating company, owning the valuable pieces, and your banker noticed the clean structure: your borrowing line just got friendlier.
actions.narrative_failure: The entity is formed and the registered agent is in place, but the operating agreement and asset transfers are still being finalized. The shell is up; the protection firms up as you complete the paperwork.

actions.label: Get Business Insurance
actions.description: One stack of business-paid cover: income protection, critical-illness, key-person, and liability.
actions.lesson: Protect the engine before you build on it: cover your income if you can't work, replace yourself for the company, and absorb lawsuits. One tax nuance worth knowing — for disability/income cover you pick ONE: deduct the premiums (then benefits are taxable) OR pay with after-tax dollars (then benefits come tax-free). You can't have both.
actions.narrative_success: The stack is in force, all on the business books. Income protection covers your family if you can't work, critical & chronic illness riders pay you a tax-free lump sum if your health gives out, the key-person payout would fund your replacement, and the liability policy stands between a lawsuit and everything you've built. Premiums run about $550/mo.
actions.narrative_failure: Applications submitted; underwriting wants more medical records on one. The other two are active. Should fully clear next month.

actions.label: Set a Monthly Tax Reserve
actions.description: Automatically set aside a slice of revenue each month for taxes — so the quarterly estimated payments the IRS expects (and the year-end bill) stop being a surprise. The reserve sits in a money-market account earning ~4%/yr while it waits.
actions.lesson: Set aside taxes every month and tax season stops being a crisis. That money was never yours — treat it that way and you'll never get blindsided. Later, when you open a cash-value policy you can fold this reserve into it: there it grows faster and tax-free, and you simply borrow against it (tax-free) to pay the IRS when the bill comes.
actions.narrative_success: Tax reserve is active. Every month, a slice of revenue goes into a separate pot. Future you will be grateful.
actions.narrative_failure: Set up started but your bookkeeping needs cleanup first. Should be running next month.

actions.label: Open a Solo 401(k) / SEP
actions.description: Set up an owner retirement plan. Each month a slice of profit is swept in PRE-TAX — so the real cash cost is only what's left after the deduction — and it compounds tax-deferred until you retire. A simple, legal way to shelter income the IRS would otherwise take now.
actions.lesson: A Solo 401(k) or SEP-IRA lets an owner shelter a big chunk of income PRE-TAX — a Solo 401(k) allows ~$23k of employee deferral plus employer contributions up to ~$69k total; a SEP is ~25% of comp. It's a DEFERRAL: you skip the tax now and pay it when you withdraw in retirement (or choose Roth — pay tax now, withdraw tax-free later). Because the contribution is deductible, sheltering $1 only costs you about 75¢ of real cash, and the full dollar compounds tax-deferred. High earners can later stack a cash-balance / defined-benefit plan to shelter $100k+/yr.
actions.narrative_success: Your Solo 401(k) is open. From here a slice of profit sweeps in pre-tax every month — the deduction covers part of the cost, and the balance compounds tax-deferred into a retirement nest egg you own.

actions.label: Fund a Cash-Value Policy
actions.description: Open an IUL and deploy your first $10,000 into it (~$9,800 lands as cash value after the ~2% cost of insurance); ongoing funding then flows automatically from your monthly cash flow. Heads-up: like a real policy it carries a ~$10,000 surrender charge in the early years, so you can't borrow against it until you've funded the cash value well above that. This is your capital — Epic Life can't cover it.
actions.lesson: This is your tax-advantaged money engine — but it rewards patience. Your $10,000 is CAPITAL you deploy into the policy (not a fee); most becomes cash value you still control. The catch a real IUL has too: a SURRENDER CHARGE (~$10k, phasing out over ~10 years) means your first years of cash value are locked — you must fund it WELL ABOVE the charge before you can borrow a cent against it. So build income and credit FIRST, open it early, and fund it steadily; once the cash value clears the surrender charge you can borrow up to ~90% of it anytime with no bank approval. Don't overfund past IRS limits or it becomes a 'MEC' and loses the tax-free borrowing.
actions.narrative_success: Policy in force and monthly funding is on. You deployed $10,000 to open it — about $9,800 is now in the cash value after the ~2% cost of insurance, growing tax-deferred (index-linked, capped upside, 0% floor), and automatic contributions keep feeding it. You can borrow against up to 90% of it anytime — no bank approval needed.
actions.narrative_failure: Underwriting needs more info. Should be approved next month.

actions.label: Activate Tax-Free Passive Income
actions.description: Turn on a monthly tax-free income stream drawn against your policy's cash value — which keeps compounding while the loan nets from the death benefit.
actions.lesson: This is the crown jewel — but it's a discipline, not magic. You draw tax-free income as loans against your policy while the cash value keeps compounding, and the loans are settled from the death benefit, not your pocket. It works ONLY while the policy stays funded and the loan balance stays well under the cash value — overborrow (or a few bad index years) and the policy can lapse, which turns every deferred gain into a taxable bill. Managed well, the spread funds income for life; managed carelessly, it collapses.
actions.narrative_success: The income switch is on. Each month the insurer wires you a tax-free check drawn against your cash value — and your policy keeps compounding as if you never touched it. You're being paid to own the asset.

actions.label: Borrow Against Your Policy
actions.description: Borrow up to 90% against your policy's cash value — no credit check, no bank approval — while the cash value keeps growing.
actions.lesson: You just accessed cash tax-free — no bank, no credit check — and your money kept compounding as if you never touched it. This is how the wealthy spend without selling assets or triggering a tax bill. Two conditions keep it tax-free: the policy must stay in force (don't let loans exceed the cash value) and it must not be overfunded into a 'MEC.' Within those lines, you're your own bank.
actions.narrative_success: Policy loan approved instantly — no credit check, no application. The money is in your account tomorrow, and your cash value keeps compounding as if you never touched it.

actions.label: Buy Rental Property
actions.description: Buy a rental or small commercial property with the bank's money — monthly cashflow, appreciation, and tax benefits while the tenant pays it down.
actions.lesson: This is leverage. You control a six-figure income-producing asset with a fraction of your own cash — the bank funds the rest, the tenant pays it down, and it appreciates while you sleep. Paying all cash would be the INEFFICIENT move.
actions.narrative_success: Keys in hand. The duplex cash-flows $700/month after mortgage, taxes, and insurance. Passive income is real now.
actions.narrative_failure: The property closed, but the inspection found issues. Repairs ate into your projections. It'll cash-flow, just not as much.

actions.label: Velocity Banking 👑
actions.description: Epic Life perk. Run your household through a line of credit and sweep every surplus dollar at your debt — paying down a mortgage or revolving balance years faster. You set the aggressiveness; chunk extra anytime from the ⚡ panel, no turn needed.
actions.lesson: The real engine here is your SURPLUS cash flow, not the line itself — velocity banking just forces every spare dollar at principal, so an amortizing debt pays down years faster and equity builds quicker. With no surplus it does nothing, and a higher-rate line can cost more than it saves. You could lump-sum that surplus straight at the loan and get the same result; the line mainly adds discipline (and risk if you lean on it). Powerful while cash-flow positive; it bites if you go negative or the line gets frozen in a downturn.
actions.narrative_success: Velocity banking is on. Your surplus now sweeps straight at your target debt every month — and you can chunk extra whenever cash flow allows, from the ⚡ Velocity panel on your dashboard.
actions.narrative_failure: You set up the line, but the paperwork hit a snag this month.

actions.label: SBA Loan
actions.description: Apply for a government-backed SBA loan — the cheapest, longest-term growth capital a small business can get. Harder to qualify for (they scrutinize your cash flow), but the rate and terms beat any credit line.
actions.lesson: The SBA guarantees part of the loan, so the bank lends more, longer, and cheaper than it otherwise would. It's the best growth debt a small business can access — if your cash flow can service it. Lenders underwrite hard on debt-to-income, so keep DTI low.
actions.narrative_success: Approved. The SBA guarantee unlocked a large, low-rate, long-term loan — the best money your business has access to.
actions.narrative_failure: The SBA application stalled — underwriting flagged your debt-to-income as too tight to service a loan this size. Pay down debt or grow revenue, then reapply.

actions.label: Finance Equipment (Section 179)
actions.description: Use the bank's money to buy equipment that lifts your capacity — then write off the whole cost this year with Section 179. Productive debt plus an instant tax deduction.
actions.lesson: Good debt working twice: the equipment raises what your business can deliver, and Section 179 lets you deduct the whole purchase the year you place it in service — so the tax savings pay for a chunk of it. (It's capped to annual limits and can only offset business profit, not create a loss.) Far better than paying cash and depreciating slowly.
actions.narrative_success: Equipment financed and in service. Capacity is up — and the Section 179 write-off just cut this year's tax bill.
actions.narrative_failure: The equipment financing fell through this month.

actions.label: Merchant Cash Advance ⚠️
actions.description: Need cash fast with no credit check? An MCA wires it today — but you repay a fixed amount far larger than you borrowed, skimmed daily from your sales. The most expensive money there is.
actions.lesson: An MCA isn't a loan — it buys your future sales at a steep discount. A 1.4 'factor rate' means borrowing $20k costs you $28k, and because it's repaid daily over just 6–12 months the effective APR is often well over 80–100%. This is what bad credit forces you into. Build credit so you never need it.
actions.narrative_success: The cash hit your account within hours — no credit check, no questions. The repayment, skimmed from every day's sales, is about to teach you why this is a last resort.

actions.label: Set Up a Living Trust
actions.description: Put your assets in a revocable living trust — your estate skips probate, stays private, and passes smoothly to your family. The first step of a real asset-protection and legacy plan.
actions.lesson: A revocable living trust avoids probate (faster, private, cheaper for your heirs) — but because you can still revoke it, it does NOT shield assets from lawsuits or estate tax while you're alive. That protection takes irrevocable trusts, which come later. Think of this as the estate-planning starting point, not asset protection yet.
actions.narrative_success: Your living trust is in place. Your estate will skip probate — private, smooth, and ready to hold the assets you're building.

actions.label: Form a Captive Insurance Co.
actions.description: Start your own insurance company to cover risks your commercial policies don't. Your business pays it deductible premiums — and under §831(b) those premiums accumulate inside the captive almost tax-free, as reserves you own.
actions.lesson: The wealthy self-insure on purpose: the operating company deducts the premiums (lowering taxable income), and the captive keeps them nearly tax-free under §831(b) (premiums capped ~$2.8M/yr). In low-claim years that money compounds as YOUR reserves — a deductible expense becomes a tax-advantaged, protected asset. The catch is real: it must be genuine insurance with real risk distribution across multiple insureds — the IRS treats abusive 'captives' as a listed transaction with steep penalties, so this only works documented and legitimate.
actions.narrative_success: Your captive is licensed and bound. Each month your business pays it deductible premiums that build tax-advantaged reserves you own — sheltering income, protecting assets, and quietly compounding into real wealth.
